EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front produced hevy snow for the Southwest Washington Cascades. Cold Air in the Columbia Gorge resulted in heavy snow as a front associated with an atmospheric river produced precipitation across Southwest Washington. Washington Department of Transportation closed Highway 14 through the Columbia River Gorge between mileposts 18 and 43 due to the snow causing multiple vehicle spinouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters reported 5 to 8 inches of snow around Skamania near Highway 14 the morning of December 1.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 1-2 foot snow totals were observed across the South Washington Cascades  for elevations above 3000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front resulted in high winds along the Southwest Washington Coast on December 2, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Transportation Wind sensor located on the Megler Bridge between the Oregon and Washington in Astoria, Oregon reported a peak wind of 55 mph with a gust of 70 mph.  A wind sensor at Cape Disappointment measured wind gusts of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain in the South Washington Cascade foothills. The Wind River Valley appears to have received 0.50 to 1.00 inch of ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred in the south Washington Cascade foothills, including the Wind River Valley, which appears to have received 0.50 to 1.00 inch of ice. A trained spotter measured 0.10 inch of ice at 1300 ft elevation near Underwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains on December 5th which led to minor river and urban flooding across portions of southwest Washington. Clark County and Cowlitz County saw landslides, along with minor river flooding, including the Cowlitz River at Kelso and the Grays River near Rosburg. Portions of the Willapa Hills and south Washington Cascades received 12 to 16 inches of rain over a 5 day period beginning December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AMTRAK rail experienced a small landslide on their track between mile marker 129 and 130.5 near Felida and canceled the train between Seattle and Portland through December 7th. No impacts other than their internal transportation delays occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river brought heavy rains on December 5th which led to minor river and urban flooding across portions of southwest Washington. Clark County and Cowlitz County saw landslides, along with minor river flooding, including the Cowlitz River at Kelso and the Grays River near Rosburg. Portions of the Willapa Hills and south Washington Cascades received 12 to 16 inches of rain over a 5 day period beginning December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays River rose above flood stage on December 4th, cresting at 16.24 feet on December 5th at 9 am, and fell below flood stage on December 6th. Flood stage is 12.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moved through Eastern Washington and brought scattered thunderstorms. The main areas stretched along Highway 2 from Waterville Plateau to Northern Spokane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported landspout near Wilbur Airport. No damage reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willapa River at Willapa rose reached minor flood stage during a period of 1 and half hours, cresting at 21.03 feet at 315 pm on December 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure of the coast of Oregon combined with high pressure inland resulted in gusty easterly winds from the western Columbia River Gorge. East wind impacted the Portland & Vancouver metro region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of trees toppled in the Vancouver and Woodland areas because of wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent front with an associated low pressure moved off the Pacific Ocean into northwest Oregon. This resulted in high winds at the coast as well as windy conditions for inland valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind of 77 mph at Cape Disappointment (82SW1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent front with an associated low pressure moved off the Pacific Ocean into northwest Oregon. This resulted in high winds at the coast as well as windy conditions for inland valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts occurred at Huckleberry RAWS (HKFW1), 68 mph and Abernathy RAWS (ABNW1), 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt led to a debris along Mad River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm heavy rains led to logs and debris flushing down the Mad River above Pine Flats campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front swept across the region, followed by rapidly lowering snow levels affecting the Cascades as well as the Coast Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 2 to 6 inches of snow between Castle Rock and Kalama.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm brought heavy rains to the area. Rain and snow melt led to a debris flow near the 25 Mile Creek Burn Scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris flow and flooding near Eagle Creek off the Twisp River. Several large logs and mud moved down the channel and impacted the forest service roads. Rain, for areas around the spot, several days prior ranged from 0.30-1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire started from unknown causes on June 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Matson Wildfire started on June 24 from unknown causes. It burned 666 acres of grassland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm generated a funnel cloud near Quincy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media photo showed funnel cloud near Quincy. It was in an isolated area. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought high winds to the Wenatchee area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Entiat raws recorded a gust 58 MPH at 2035LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much coler air infiltrated Western Washington at the end of February, after a relatively quiet three quarters of the month. The heaviest snow fell between the 25th and 26th, with reports of over 4 inches across many lowland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow fell across the Kitsap Peninsula late in the day on Feb 25 and continued through the morning hours on February 26th. In general, most snowfall reports ranged from 1 inch or less, but areas nearing the Hood Canal reported around 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much coler air infiltrated Western Washington at the end of February, after a relatively quiet three quarters of the month. The heaviest snow fell between the 25th and 26th, with reports of over 4 inches across many lowland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow fell across the Hood Canal area late in the day on Feb 25 and continued through the morning hours of the Feb 26th. Reports of 4-7 inches were recorded across portions of the Hood Canal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much coler air infiltrated Western Washington at the end of February, after a relatively quiet three quarters of the month. The heaviest snow fell between the 25th and 26th, with reports of over 4 inches across many lowland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across the Southwest Interior late in the day on Feb 25th through the morning hours on Feb 26th. In general, most reports of new snow ranged from 2 inches or less, however, a few  reports of 5 inches were reported.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front entered Western Washington, bringing another round of heavy mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations show that during a 12 hour period in the Central Cascades, within a 12 hour period, 12 inches of snow fell when a Winter Storm Warning was in place. Totals also increased afterwards due to the presence of the Puget Sound Convergence Zone.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the higher terrain of Western Washington in mid-late February as a storm system moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the northern Cascades between Feb 19-21, with the heaviest falling from late in the day on the 20th into the morning hours of the 21st. The Mount Baker Ski area reported about 18 inches of new snow within a 24 hours period from 8am on the 20th to 8am on the 21st. Snow then tapered through the morning and into the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gap winds developed across the Cascades on January 9th. Winds in the gaps were gusting to 40-45 mph, including in Fall City, WA where a tree was downed and created a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Training Academy RAWS station near Fall City, WA reported a peak gust of 45 mph the morning of the 9th. The gusts downed a tree which killed a women as she walking on her property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought strong winds to the WA Coast on January, with the central coast experiencing the strongest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A station at Westport along the central WA Coast reported a sustained win of 54 mph and a gust of 67 mph around 2pm on 1/21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper-level trough and cold frontal boundary brought periods of heavy snow to the eastern Mountains followed by a period of favorable up-slope flow later on Feb 5th into the 6th once the trough axis passed over the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site TOHW1(Touchet) recorded 12 inches over a 24 hour period ending at 0000 on Feb 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough and strong cold-frontal boundary pushed through the region the morning of Feb 13th followed by a period of post-frontal showers the rest of the 13th driven by anomalous cold air aloft. A secondary wave feature dropping southward from BC the night of the 13th into the 14th provided continued snow showers over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site TOHW1(Touchet) measured 12 inches of snow over a 15 hour period ending at 0300 on Feb 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A season of heavy snow in the Okanogan Valley created snow load issues for some structures.  Snowfall accumulations in the area were near 56 inches. Snow depths were around 27 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow load across the area collapsed several buildings. These buildings were out buildings or garages and not inhabited. It is believed to be around 5 structures lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and near half an inch of rain created some localized flooding and rock slides in the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peter Dan road closed after hillside sloughed onto road half a mile from Highway 155.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer recorded 6.8 inches of snow 4 miles Southwest of East Wenatchee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow supported by a strong polar jet in conjunction with a cold frontal boundary facilitated a sustained period of light to moderate snowfall as well as breezy to gusty winds late on February 20th through the 21st and 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Tri-Cities Airport, WA measured a wind gust of 68mph at 1913 PST on Feb. 20th. The mesonet station DW9572 Benton city 4 miles northwest of West Richland, WA measured a wind gust of 60mph at 2000 PST on Feb. 20th.

While most locations in Eastern Washington received one to three inches, a few locations reached six to ten inches. The trough exited the region by April 4 and the snow shower threat diminished.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer estimated eight inches of snow in Waverly. Another report of six inches was estimated in Latah. Whitman County snow amounts ranged from one to four inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate atmospheric river brought steady rain to southwest Washington April 9-10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river that brought heavy rain caused a landslide that sent debris across I-5 north of Woodland, WA near milepost 24. Around 2.2 inches of rain fell in the Woodland area. Northbound lanes on I-5 were closed for more than 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions and clearing skies allowed for temperatures to drop below freezing across portions of the Columbia Basin and and foothills of the Blue Mountains the morning of April 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station GW2790 Starbuck reported at to below freezing temperatures between 0505 to 0655 PDT on April 15th. The mesonet station DW6278 Walla Walla reported 0401 to 0731 PDT on April 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent negative tilted upper trough supported by a strong upper-level jet moved down along the British Columbia coastline into the PacNW and brought heavy snow to the Cascades and Blue Mountains of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Touchet SNOTEL measured 11in of snow over 12 hours starting at at 2200PDT on the 31st of March through 1000PDT on April 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stubborn cold air and dry conditions near the surface resulted in the Yakima valley experiencing freezing morning low temperatures on April 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KYKM at the Yakima Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0530 to 0645 PDT on April 21st. The mesonet station AT095 Tieton reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0420 to 0730 PDT on April 21st. Other mesonet stations located across the Yakima valley, including farmland, reported temperatures at to below freezing for over an hour during the morning of April 21st.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SLOPES OF THE EST WASHINGTON CASCADE CREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent negative tilted upper trough supported by a strong upper-level jet moved down along the British Columbia coastline into the PacNW and brought heavy snow to the Cascades and Blue Mountains of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stampede Pass SNOTEL measured 14 inches of snow over 29 hours starting at 2000PDT on March 31st to 0100PDT on April 2nd, and the the White Pass E.S. SNOTEL measured 12 inches of snow over 41 hours starting at 1700PDT on March 31st to 1000PDT on April 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions and clearing skies allowed for temperatures to drop below freezing across portions of the Columbia Basin and and foothills of the Blue Mountains the morning of April 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KPSC at the Tri-Cities airport reported at to below freezing temperatures between 0345 to 0625 PDT on April 15th. The mesonet station AW092 (K7KOG) Kennewick reported at to below freezing temperatures between 0312 to 0418 PDT on April 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air advection behind a cold front passage the previous day, in combination with clearing skies overnight led to temperatures in the Kittitas and Yakima valleys dropping to below freezing in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KELN at the Bowers Field Airport reported temperatures at or below freezing between 0430 to 0752 PDT on April 17th. The Washington DOT station TRYEG at Ryegrass reported temperatures at or below freezing between 0107 to 0752 on April 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air advection behind a cold front passage the previous day, in combination with clearing skies overnight led to temperatures in the Kittitas and Yakima valleys dropping to below freezing in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site KF7MKL Union Gap measured temperatures at or below freezing between 0415 to 0745 PDT on April 17th. The mesonet station KK7GO-7 Yakima measured temperatures at to below freezing between 0500 to 0745 PDT on April 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system taking on a negative tilt offshore the PacNW continued to advect a cooler airmass into the region, resulting in morning low temperatures dropping to below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KELN at Bowers Field Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0353 to 0715 PDT on April 18th. The mesonet station DW9732 Ellensburg reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0145 to 0730 on April 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system taking on a negative tilt offshore the PacNW continued to advect a cooler airmass into the region, resulting in morning low temperatures dropping to below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station KK7GO-7 Yakima reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0345 to 0636 PDT on April 18th. The mesonet station W7YKM-3 Tieton reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0420 to 0700 PDT on April 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent negatively tilted trough over the PacNW conintued to usher in a cooler airmass in the region. The cooler air infiltrated the rest of the Basin and Blue Mountain foothills, resulting in at to below freezing temperatures in these areas in addition to the Yakima and Kittitas valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KELN at Bowers Field Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0500 to 0615 PDT on April 19th. The mesonet station DW9732 Ellensburg reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0000 to 0745 PDT on April 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent negatively tilted trough over the PacNW conintued to usher in a cooler airmass in the region. The cooler air infiltrated the rest of the Basin and Blue Mountain foothills, resulting in at to below freezing temperatures in these areas in addition to the Yakima and Kittitas valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station DW4698 Prescott reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0000 to 0546 PDT on April 19th. The mesonet station EW4185 Lowden reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0100 to 0713 PDT on April 19th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather persisted from the end of March into the beginning of April, bringing late winter snow to the mountains. While all Cascade and Olympic mountain zones experienced fresh snowfall, the southern Cascades received the most.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow carried over from the end of March, resulting in hefty snow accumulation at Paradise (and nearby locations) from April 1-2. From 12 AM April 1 to 12AM April 2, around 20 inches of new snow had fallen (and about 12 inches of snow feel in a 12 hour period ending at 12PM on the 1st). Snow, heavy at times, then persisted through most of the 2nd before tapering into the early morning hours of the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern continuing through early April brought a round of windy conditions, mountain snow, and heavy lowland rain to Western Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS on Whidbey Island recorded a wind gust of 58 mph at 12 PM and 62 mph at 1PM on April 9. Nearby locations through Northern Puget Sound, Admiralty Inlet, the San Juan Islands, and Western Whatcom and Skagit Counties reported breezy conditions, however, this ASOS site recorded the highest wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent cold airmass near the surface and clear skies in the morning resulted in freezing morning lows in the Eastern Columbia River Gorge, areas of the Blue Mountain Foothills, and the Yakima/Kittitas valleys the morning of April 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KELN at Bowers Field Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0100 to 0745 PDT on April 20th. The mesonet station DW9732 Ellensburg reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0130 to 0815 PDT on April 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent cold airmass near the surface and clear skies in the morning resulted in freezing morning lows in the Eastern Columbia River Gorge, areas of the Blue Mountain Foothills, and the Yakima/Kittitas valleys the morning of April 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KYKM at the Yakima Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0000 to 0730 PDT on April 20th. The AWOS K1S5 at the Sunnyside Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0055 to 0715 PDT on April 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent cold airmass near the surface and clear skies in the morning resulted in freezing morning lows in the Eastern Columbia River Gorge, areas of the Blue Mountain Foothills, and the Yakima/Kittitas valleys the morning of April 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KDLS at the Columbia Gorge Regional Airport reported temperatures at to below freezing between 0600 to 0705 PDT on April 20th. Other mesonet stations across the Washington side of the Columbia Gorge also reported below freezing temperatures for over an hour.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy mountain snow was experienced in the North Cascades around mid-April as an active weather pattern persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow accumulation began on April 15 around noon, resulting in generally less than 1 inch into April 16th at the Mt Baker Ski Area. Snow really began to pickup on April 16th (between 5am-7am), with well over a foot of snow falling by the early morning hours of the 17th (5am-7am). Snow also then continued through the 18th, with about another half a foot falling in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous weather system brings heavy mountain snow to the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations show that now started late on February 7th into the late afternoon hours on February 8th, with around 9 to 11 inches falling within a 12 hour period, reaching Winter Weather Advisory criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt during a period of unseasonably warm temperatures resulted in a debris flow that washed out a bridge on Spirit Lake Memorial Highway (State Route 504.)  Twelve people visiting Johnson Ridge Observatory were trapped by the blockage and were evacuated by air rescue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spirit Lake Outlet Bridge on State Route 504 near milepost 43 was washed out by rock, mud, ice, and water around 9 pm, blocking access to the Johnston Ridge Observatory. Twelve people and one dog were airlifted from one side of the slide to the other as there are no alternate routes. The slide caused power outages in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much coler air infiltrated Western Washington at the end of February, after a relatively quiet three quarters of the month. The heaviest snow fell between the 25th and 26th, with reports of over 4 inches across many lowland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRAHs stations from multiple locations across Western Whatcom County suggested that moderate snowfall occurred late in the day on Feb 25 through the morning of Feb 26. Sumas reported the highest measurable snowfall at 8.7 inches, while most areas around and north of Bellingham up towards the WA/Canada border reported 1-4 inches.||Additional snowfall was measured again from Feb 27 into the 28th, with a few reports of around 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter weather event impacted portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Particularly hard hit was the Portland and Vancouver metro area. A crippling snowstorm impacted the Portland and Vancouver metro area on the afternoon of February 22, continuing into the night. The storm brought widespread 6 to 12 inch snowfall totals to the area. Snowfall distribution was greatly influenced by mesoscale banding. The timing of this event also magnified its impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wide variation in snowfall totals, ranging from a couple inches near Cascade Locks to over a foot closer to Rooster Rock, Corbett and Washougal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter weather event impacted portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington. Particularly hard hit was the Portland and Vancouver metro area. A crippling snowstorm impacted the Portland and Vancouver metro area on the afternoon of February 22, continuing into the night. The storm brought widespread 6 to 12 inch snowfall totals to the area. Snowfall distribution was greatly influenced by mesoscale banding. The timing of this event also magnified its impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began to around 0900 PST, but the heaviest snow fell during the afternoon through early evening hours. Around 3 to 7 inches of snow fell in the Vancouver metro area by 2230 PST. The axis of heaviest snow was just east of the WFO, extending northward from roughly Gresham, OR and across eastern Clark County (WA) where up to 12 inches of snow fell.  This resulted in snarled traffic and numerous traffic accidents. Many vehicles were caught up in the traffic jam well into the night. Schools and government offices were closed for up to two days following the event. A Clark County Sheriff was injured when a snow heavy tree fell on his vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluding Gulf of Alaska frontal system moved across SW Washington and NW Oregon,|with mixed rain and snow for the interior lowlands. Upslope W-NW flow behind the cold front led to periods of heavy snow in the Coast Range and Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 3 to 7 inches of snow fell per NOHRSC and CoCoRaHS analysis.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations across Western Skagit County recorded an extended period of above normal temperatures, with afternoon highs generally ranging from the low to upper 80s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms impacted the region bringing damage and flash flooding to Eastern Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A several day period of continuous rain caused a rock slide along Highway 20 near Sherman Pass. Rainfall amounts from 0.9 to 1.1 inches fell from November 1 through November 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSDOT cleared a rockslide on Highway 20 at MP320. No mention of how big the slide was.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave with low level jet support pushed through the region on November 11. It brought strong winds across the Inland Northwest. The Eastern Cascade Valleys and the Columbia Basin received gusts in the range of 40 to 55 MPH. The Northern Mountain areas show winds in the 20 to 30 MPH. Around 1800 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station DW6220 located 2 miles West of Pullman recorded a gust of 59 MPH at 1330PM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spokane Airport recorded a gust of 52 MPH at 1246LST. A Coop observer 3 miles Northwest of Spokane reported a gust of 55 MPH at 1201LST. The local utility company reported 1000 customers without power in Spokane County at 1240LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of light drizzle, freezing temperatures, and dense freezing fog caused light ice accumulation on the area roadways across the Central Columbia Basin. It lead to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog around Othello led to icy road conditions and caused two accidents. First accident occurred 4 miles West of Othello on state route 26. Vehicle lost control and landed in ditch injuring 5 occupants. Second accident occurred state route 17 North of Othello. Tanker crashed and released 10000 gallons of fuel causing closure of the roadway through 1738LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low passed through the Pacific Northwest and brought the first round of widespread snow to Eastern Washington. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer recorded 1.9 inches of snow at 12.2 miles Northwest of Entiat, WA at 0600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing northerly flow reinforced by a cold arctic airmass behind a shortwave trough passage starting October 26th lead to temperatures region-wide dropping well below normal. Overnight temperatures across the lower elevations in the intermountain Pacific Northwest were able to cool well below freezing across the region for much of the overnight period. This lead to all lower elevation zones experiencing their end of season freeze by the morning of October 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS KDLS recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 9 hours ending at 0945 PDT. The Mesonet station FW7259 recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 12 hours ending at 0941 PDT. The WA Department of Transportation station TMARY recorded temperatures at or below 32 degrees for approximately 5 hours ending at 0823 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool, unstable airmass moved across the region resulting in an EF-0 tornado touching down in La Center, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado confirmed by video appeared on the ground for less than 1 minute in La Center, WA. Trash cans and a basketball hoop were blown over, along with a few roof shingles seen swirling in the air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low and associated front approached the north Oregon and south Washington coast. High winds were observed on the immediate coast around the Columbia River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 68 mph reported at Cape Disappointment (82SW1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Crater Creek Wildfire on August 18. It continued through September 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Crater Creek Wildfire near the North end of Pasayten Wilderness and Loomis DNR. It burned 5145 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming system brought strong winds and dry air into the Spokane Area. Two wildfires were started by unknown human causes and quickly spread through residential areas. A total of 270 structures were lost or damaged.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SLOPES OF THE EST WASHINGTON CASCADE CREST
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter weather systems impacted the Pacific Northwest between December 1st through the 3rd. While the systems moving through were fairly quick, they were able to produce heavy snow accumulations across many of the mountain zones across the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bumping Ridge SNOTEL site measured 14 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 2AM PST on Dec. 2nd. A trained spotter 25 miles southwest of South Cle Elum measured 15 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending around 9AM PST on Dec. 2nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Touchet SNOTEL site measured 14 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending 10AM PST on Dec. 2nd. The Ski Bluewood resort measured 12 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 8:15 PM PST on Dec. 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves tracked across Eastern Washington bringing several days of light to moderate snow fall. Snowfall was heaviest in mountains with amounts ranging from 10 to 20 inches. The low lands excluding Okanogan Valley received amounts ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Okanogan Valley and Okanogan Highlands were out of the high moisture areas and received only a couple of inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prior to 7 AM, a 9-year-old was killed in a crash just south of Warden after the vehicle he was traveling in hit a patch of ice and spun off the roadway. The three other occupants sustained non-life-threatening injuries. An estimated 3 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves tracked across Eastern Washington bringing several days of light to moderate snow fall. Snowfall was heaviest in mountains with amounts ranging from 10 to 20 inches. The low lands excluding Okanogan Valley received amounts ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Okanogan Valley and Okanogan Highlands were out of the high moisture areas and received only a couple of inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer 2 miles south of Anatone recorded 9.3 inches of snow for December 1-4. December 1 received two tenths. Another 2.3 was added on December 2. December 3 received 5.8 inches. Another one inch was added on December 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with a warm air push brought another round of snow to the region. Mountains received another 12 inches of snow. The Basin and valleys received a range of 3 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 7 inches of snow 9 miles west of Twisp. Other reports in the area ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer recorded  8 inches of  snow for the  period. The highest amount on December 2 with 5 inches. The other days saw one inch of  snow.
